texts.orig: A B S T R A C T
texts.text: A B S T R A C T

texts.label: text
texts.orig: A facile method to improve CO2 separation performance of polymer is to incorporate CO2-philic particles. In this study, sulfonated polymer brush functionalized graphene oxide nanosheets (S-GO) as fi llers, with sulfonated polymer brush as CO2-philic chain anchored on GO were introduced into SPEEK membrane at nanoscale level to enhance CO2 selectivity of the membrane. The S-GO enhanced the interface compatibility as shown by SEM, and the ' sieve-in-a-cage ' morphology in MMMs disappeared by the introduction of CO2-philic polymer brush. Bene fi ting from the introduction of brush at the interface, on one hand, the CO2-philic brush on S-GO helped to widen CO2 transport pathways veri fi ed by the increased radius of free volume cavity ( r 3) and the fractional free volume (FFV), which resulted in the increase in gas permeability. On the other hand, the addition of the polymer brush on GO increased CO2-philic sites, which imparted enhancement in the CO2 solubility selectivity. Besides, the transport of gases through the GO nanosheets created the increment in CO2 di ff usion selectivity. SPEEK/SGO MMMs showed pronounced enhancements in both CO2 permeability and CO2/CH4(N2) selectivity. Speci fi cally, for the SPEEK/S-GO membrane doped with 8 wt% S-GO nanosheets, the CO2 permeability reached 1327 Barrer, and CO2/CH4 selectivity increased by 179% in comparison with the SPEEK control membrane, surpassing the 2008 Robeson upper bound. The distinctive virtues of polymer brush functionalized fi ller may be extended to fabricate a series of MMMs for e ffi cient molecule separation.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Several recent researches have proved that polymer brush modi fi ed materials intrigued a variety of separation applications due to unique attributes such as su ffi cient length of chain, high functional groups content and fl exible mobility of chain [36 -40]. The polymer brush was introduced into membrane, which showed promising for gas separation. Baker et al. illuminated the in fl uence of side-chain length of polymer brush membranes on the gas permeability and CO2/H2 selectivity, and investigated that side-chains caused the polymer brush membrane to retain relatively high CO2/H2 selectivity of 12 [41]. Dory et al. reported a polymer brush that could be reversibly converted between stretched and shortened states by crossing CO2 and N2 alternately [42]. Wu et al. [43] prepared polymer brushes functionalized organic microcapsules to fabricate MMMs, and the MMMs showed potential in the CO2 capture.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The introduction of polar -SO3 sites into membranes showed potential in CO2 separation [44]. SPEEK polymer with abundant sulfonic groups exhibited hydrophilic characteristics, and assimilated water which was favorable to gas transport resulting in remarkably improved gas permeability [21]. In this study, MMMs loaded with sulfonated polymer brush functionalized graphene oxide (S-GO) nanosheets were designated to fabricate gas separation membrane. The sulfonic acid groups both from S-GO nanosheets and polymer matrix were explored to build uninterrupted CO2 transport pathways and enhance CO2 solubility selectivity. Graphene oxide nanosheets were expected to create more tortuous pathways for CH4 or N2 as larger molecule, causing the enhancement of the di ff usivity selectivity. The e ff ect of sulfonated polymer brush functionalized graphene oxide nanosheets content on CO2 selectivity of membranes was explored for CO2/CH4 and CO2/N2 systems.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 2.2. Preparation of the sulfonated polymer brush functionalized graphene oxide
texts.text: 2.2. Preparation of the sulfonated polymer brush functionalized graphene oxide

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Exfoliated GO nanosheets were obtained by our previous literature using the modi fi ed Hummers' method [45]. Using GO as the starting material, the S-GO was prepared via a non-crosslinked precipitation polymerization method [17], and the preparation process was shown in Scheme 1: GO (0.5 g) was dispersed into the ammonia water (1.5 mL) and ethanol (18 mL) mixed solution. Then, MPS (0.2 mL) was dropped into the above-mentioned mixed solution under stirring to get GO-MPS. Successively, the GO-MPS was puri fi ed and dried. GO-MPS (0.15 g), AIBN (0.01 g), SS (0.25 mL), and St (0.25 mL) were added into acetonitrile (40 mL) in a round-bottom fl ask, and the temperature of mixture in round-bottom fl ask was elevated to boiling state and held for 20 min. Afterward, the precipitation in round-bottom fl ask was puri fi ed and dispersed in 0.1 M hydrochloric acid solution to replace the Na + ion with H + ion. Finally, the sulfonated polymer brush functionalized GO was prepared and designated as S-GO.

Finally, the sulfonated polymer brush functionalized GO was prepared and designated as S-GO.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 2.3. Preparation of S-GO loaded MMMs
texts.text: 2.3. Preparation of S-GO loaded MMMs

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The S-GO loaded MMMs were fabricated by the following steps. A certain amount of SPEEK powder was dispersed into DMAc at 25 °C to obtain 10 wt % SPEEK solution. GO or S-GO nanosheets were dispersed in DMAc under ultrasonic state and then mixed with the above SPEEK solution with a stirrer. The GO(S-GO)-SPEEK mixture was cast onto glass surface vessel, kept at 60 °C for 12 h and 80 °C for 12 h in succession. The MMMs were named after SPEEK/GO-X or SPEEK/S-GO-X, where GO or S-GO denoted the fi ller, and -X represented weight ratio of GO or S-GO to SPEEK. The SPEEK control membrane was fabricated using the above-mentioned method like MMMs. The thickness of the asprepared membranes was in the range of 55 -65 μ m.

texts.text: 2.4. Characterizations

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The morphology of the GO and S-GO was observed with a PHILIPS TECNAI G2 F20 transmission electron microscope (TEM). The chemical structures of GO and S-GO were conducted by Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR, Bruker Vertex 70) spectrometer. The surface elemental composition (especially sulfur on S-GO) of GO and S-GO was analyzed by a PHI-1600 X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS). The thickness of GO and S-GO was illustrated by an Agilent model 5500 AFM. GO and S-GO structures were investigated by Raman spectra collected on a HR800 Raman microscope equipped with a 514 nm laser source and UV -vis absorption spectra. An integrated GPC system (PL-GPC 50 Plus, Polymer Laboratories Ltd, UK) with a di ff erential refractive index (DRI) detector was used for the determination of average values (Mn) and broad polydispersity index (PDI), and the corresponding data of polymer brush were obtained through narrow-MWD standard calibration by application of GPC-Cirrus software (Agilent). The crystal structures of S-GO and corresponding MMMs were tested by an X-ray di ff ractor (Rigaku D/max 2500 v/pc) with scanning speed of 5 °C/min. The cross-section morphology of S-GO loaded MMMs was observed by emission scanning electron microscope (SEM, S-4800). Di ff erential scanning calorimetry (204 F1 NETZSCH) was conducted to test the glass transition temperature ( T g) of S-GO loaded MMMs. The thermal gravimetrical analysis (TGA) of S-GO and corresponding MMMs was conducted to investigate the thermal stability. The data were recorded at a heating rate of 10 °C/min from 30 to 800 °C in a nitrogen atmosphere. The area swelling of SPEEK control membrane and MMMs was tested as reported [17]. The positron annihilation lifetime spectroscopy (PALS) experiment was used to determinate the radius of free volume cavity ( r 3) and the fractional free volume (FFV) in membranes [18], and the thickness of membranes is ∼ 1.0 mm. The spectra with more than one million counts were recorded and then resolved by LT-v9 program. On assumption that the location of o-Ps occurs in a sphere potential well surrounded by an electron layer of a constant thickness Δ r (0.1656 nm), radius of free volume cavity ( r 3) was calculated from the pick-o ff annihilation lifetime of o-Ps ( τ 3) by the following semi-empirical Eq. (1).

After functionalization, S-GO had no obvious change in size and grew to 11 -15 nm in thickness. texts.orig: IR spectra, XPS spectra, XRD patterns and TGA plots were used to investigate the physical structure and chemical structure of S-GO nanosheets as shown in Fig. 3. GO nanosheets exhibited characteristic peaks at 1720 cm -1 , 1620 cm -1 , 1371 cm -1 and 1051 cm -1 , respectively. Functionalized with sulfonated polymer brush, the attribute bands of GO nanosheets became week or disappeared as shown in Fig. 3(a), implying that GO was coated by sulfonated polymer brush. The S-GO spectrum presented new characteristic bands at 1159 cm -1 and 1043 cm -1 ascribed to O=S=O stretching vibration and S -O stretching vibration bending vibration, respectively, which indicated that sulfonated polymer brush was successfully grafted on the GO. The surface elements of S-GO nanosheets were conducted by XPS (Fig. 3(b). For S-GO, the characteristic peak at 167 eV was corresponded to S2p, which was conformed to the sulfur element introduced by grafting sulfonated polymer brush. The sulfur atomic fraction in S-GO was calculated to be 9.5% (20.3% in weight), and sulfonated polymer brush functionalized GO endowed GO to reach high content amounts of sulfonic group. XRD patterns of pristine GO and S-GO were exhibited in Fig. 3(c). GO nanosheets possessed a characteristic peak with the 2 θ equal to 11.7° and the dspacing was 0.76 nm. The characteristic peak of S-GO disappeared at 11.7° and a broad peak emerged at ∼ 18°, which indicated that S-GO was disordered, attributed to the grafting of sulfonated polymer brush on GO surface. To further investigate structural characteristics of GO and S-GO, the Raman spectra were employed and shown in Fig. 3(d). The two strong peaks in the plots of both GO and SGO at around 1350 and 1600 cm -1 were corresponded to the D-band and G-band, respectively [48,49]. The intensity ratio of the D and G bands (ID/IG) could be used to characterize the degree of disorder in the GO structure. The value of ID/IG increased from 0.82 for GO to 1.03 for S-GO, indicating the increase of disorder in the composite structure due to the polymerization of polymer brush. These results also indicated the successful graft of polymer brush onto GO. The content of sulfonated polymer brush and thermal stability of S-GO were investigated by TGA curves as shown in Fig. 3(e). The sulfonated polymer brush content in SGO was measured by TGA analysis to be 21.1 wt%, which was close to the result of XPS (20.3 wt%).

texts.orig: The free volume parameters of membranes including radius of free volume cavity ( r 3), and the fractional free volume (FFV) were listed in Table 1. As the content of S-GO increased, the r 3 for S-GO doped MMMs elevated from 0.290 nm to 0.307 nm. Moreover, SPEEK/S-GO could generated higher FFV than that of SPEEK control membrane, which could bene fi t to the gas permeability. Compared with MMMs doped with pristine GO at the content of 8 wt%, the SPEEK/S-GO-8 membrane showed the decreased r 3 and increased FFV. This result indicated that polymer brush on S-GO regulated the fi ller-polymer interface and reduced the interfacial defect, which was favorable to CO2/CH4 di ff usion selectivity.

texts.orig: DSC analysis of the membranes was used to investigate polymer chain rigidi fi cation as presented in Fig. 6(a). The glass transition temperature ( T g) increased from 159.3 °C for SPEEK control membrane to 218.6 °C for SPEEK/GO-8 membrane. Moreover, the MMMs loaded with S-GO possessed a lower T g than MMMs that loaded with pristine GO membrane resulted from the repulsive interaction of sulfonic acid groups between S-GO and SPEEK, which triggered the decreased polymer chain rigidi fi cation. However, the S-GO based MMMs showed an increased T g and caused the increased chain rigidi fi cation between S-GO and SPEEK in comparison with SPEEK control membrane. To further understand the crystalline properties of SPEEK control membrane and S-GO loaded MMMs, XRD analysis was conducted (Fig. 6(b)). The SPEEK control membrane clearly showed a broad peak (10-28°). After the addition of the S-GO, the intensity of the SPEEK peak decreased in comparison with the SPEEK control membrane. The addition of S-GO disturbed the interchain packing of SPEEK due to repulsive interaction between S-GO and SPEEK, which caused decreased crystallinity and bene fi ted to the increase of gas permeability. TGA was manipulated to explore the in fl uence of the S-GO fi llers on the thermal stability of the MMMs (Fig. 6(c)). Results indicated that both SPEEK control membrane and MMMs showed a similar two-stage decomposition process. The fi rst decomposition process was caused by the evaporation of free water and bound water in both SPEEK control membrane and MMMs. The second stage began at about 440 °C, which was resulted from the degradation of the SPEEK main chain. S-GO based MMMs began to degrade at a higher temperature than that of SPEEK control membrane, which indicated the enhanced thermal stability by S-GO. The MMMs loaded with 8 wt% of GO or S-GO showed the higher thermal stability than MMMs with fi ller loading at 4 wt%, respectively,

texts.orig: Di ff usion coe ffi cient and solubility coe ffi cient of GO and S-GO doped MMMs were tested to further investigate the roles of the GO and S-GO (Table 2). As expected, both types of GO and S-GO doped MMMs showed a decreased di ff usion with increasing fi ller loading. Both the pristine GO and S-GO doped MMMs exhibited a higher CO2/CH4(N2) di ff usion selectivity than the SPEEK control membrane. The kinetic diameters of gas were in the sequence: CH4 (3.80 Å) > N2 (3.64 Å) > CO2 (3.30 Å), and the small molecule CO2 was easier to di ff use than CH4(N2) molecule in MMMs, showing the highest di ff usion coe ffi cient of 4.05 × 10 -8 cm 2 /s. Compared with the SPEEK control membrane, pristine GO and S-GO doped MMMs at the content of 8 wt% show an increased CO2/CH4(N2) di ff usion selectivity by 22%(23%) and 25%(20%), respectively. For MMMs, the solubility coe ffi cient for CO2 ( S CO2) was enhanced as GO or S-GO content increased. The S CO2 increased from 3.18 × 10 -2 cm 3 (STP)/cm 3 cmHg for SPEEK control membrane to 5.26 × 10 -2 cm 3 (STP)/cm 3 cmHg for SPEEK/S-GO at content of 8 wt %. The MMMs doped with functionalized S-GO exhibited the remarkable increase in solubility coe ffi cient compared with the unmodi fi ed GO-loaded MMMs. The enhanced solubility coe ffi cient was ascribed to the comprehensive e ff ect of enhancive CO2 -philic sites and widened CO2 transport pathways. texts.orig: 3.4. Mixed gas separation performance
texts.text: 3.4. Mixed gas separation performance

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Gas permeability was conducted using mixed gas CO2 -CH4 (70 vol% CH4) and CO2 -N2 (90 vol% N2) (Fig. 9), respectively, and e ff ect of GO or S-GO content on CO2/CH4(N2) was explored. The selectivity of mixed gas was lower than the selectivity of pure gas for both SPEEK control membrane and MMMs, which was ascribed to the competitive sorption e ff ect for mixture. However, the decreases in selectivity of pristine GO and S-GO doped MMMs were lower than that of SPEEK control membrane. The GO nanosheets interfered the con fi guration of SPEEK, and created more gas transport pathways in the polymer- fi ller interface. Moreover, S-GO with hydroxyl, carboxyl group, and sulfonic group on surface added CO2-philic sites, which resulted in the less decreased CO2/CH4(N2) selectivity.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 3.5. Influence of manipulating pressure
texts.text: 3.5. Influence of manipulating pressure

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The pressure was varied to explore the in fl uence of manipulating pressure on the gas permeability and selectivity for SPEEK control membrane, SPEEK/GO-8 and SPEEK/S-GO-8 membranes in CO2 -CH4 (70 vol% CH4) and CO2 -N2 (90 vol% N2) systems (Fig. 10). For SPEEK control membrane, the CO2 permeability increased and CO2/CH4(N2) selectivity decreased as operating pressure increased at the beginning, which was attributed to polarizable gas CO2 plasticizing mobile SPEEK polymer chain. For SPEEK/S-GO-8 membrane, CO2 permeability was almost unchanged, which indicated that S-GO nanosheets disrupted the con fi guration and restricted the mobility of SPEEK chain, engendering strong anti-plasticizing in fl uence. Moreover, interlaced segments between polymer brush on S-GO and SPEEK chain also inhibited the presence of the free volume cavity, engineering resistance to plasticization e ff ect. Therefore, SPEEK/S-GO-8 membrane was less in fl uenced by the operating pressure, indicating that the transport of CO2 through S-GO based membrane primarily complied with the solution-di ff usion mechanism at high pressure.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 4. Conclusions
texts.text: 4. Conclusions

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The CO2-philic polymer brush functionalized graphene oxide nanosheets (S-GO) were synthesized by in-situ polymerization of sulfonated polymer brush on the surface of GO, which were fi lled into SPEEK matrix to fabricate MMMs for e ffi cient CO2 separation. The resultant MMMs showed remarkable increment in CO2/CH4(N2) selectivity in comparison with those doped with pristine GO nanosheets at the same loading. On one hand, the sulfonated polymer brush as e ff ective interface modi fi er on S-GO improved interface compatibility and eliminated interface defects. On the other hand, the interlaced segments between polymer brush from S-GO nanosheets and SPEEK chain enhanced the chain rigidity of polymer matrix and widened CO2-philic pathways near the polymer- fi ller interface which elevated the CO2/ CH4(N2) selectivity. As the content of S-GO in MMMs increased, the gas permeability and CO2/CH4(N2) selectivity were improved. Particularly, the CO2 permeability and CO2/CH4(N2) selectivity of S-GO loaded humidi fi ed MMMs were 1327 Barrer and 72.2(86.4) at loading of 8 wt%, exceeding Robeson upper bound reported in 2008. The remarkably increased gas separation performance was mainly ascribed to the abundant CO2-philic sites and widened CO2 transport pathways. Besides, thermal stability of S-GO loaded MMMs was also strengthened in comparison with the SPEEK control membrane.

The resultant MMMs showed remarkable increment in CO2/CH4(N2) selectivity in comparison with those doped with pristine GO nanosheets at the same loading. On one hand, the sulfonated polymer brush as e ff ective interface modi fi er on S-GO improved interface compatibility and eliminated interface defects. On the other hand, the interlaced segments between polymer brush from S-GO nanosheets and SPEEK chain enhanced the chain rigidity of polymer matrix and widened CO2-philic pathways near the polymer- fi ller interface which elevated the CO2/ CH4(N2) selectivity. As the content of S-GO in MMMs increased, the gas permeability and CO2/CH4(N2) selectivity were improved. Particularly, the CO2 permeability and CO2/CH4(N2) selectivity of S-GO loaded humidi fi ed MMMs were 1327 Barrer and 72.2(86.4) at loading of 8 wt%, exceeding Robeson upper bound reported in 2008. The remarkably increased gas separation performance was mainly ascribed to the abundant CO2-philic sites and widened CO2 transport pathways. Besides, thermal stability of S-GO loaded MMMs was also strengthened in comparison with the SPEEK control membrane.
